🏆 Use Case of the Week

The Contract Layer

Every enterprise data architecture shares a blind spot: contracts. They're the legal and operational source code of the enterprise — and the missing domain in your semantic layer.

March 18, 2026 · By Chris O'Hara · 12 min read

Every enterprise data architecture framework published in the last decade shares a conspicuous blind spot. Whether you're looking at Gartner's data fabric reference architecture, Snowflake's data cloud positioning, or the frameworks I helped build across three books on the evolution from DMPs to CDPs to agentic platforms — the diagrams all converge on the same categories of enterprise data: customer data, operational data, behavioral data, transactional data.

What none of them represent is the single data asset that encodes how every business relationship in the enterprise actually works: contracts.

This isn't a minor omission. Contracts are the legal and operational source code of the enterprise. Revenue recognition begins with contract terms. Supplier payments flow from contractual obligations. Employment relationships, licensing structures, partnership economics, service level agreements — all of these exist first and primarily as contractual data.

9.2%
Annual revenue lost to poor contract management
$920M
Value leakage at a $10B company
$5.4B
Projected CLM market by 2030-2036

According to WorldCC (formerly the International Association for Contract and Commercial Management), poor contract management costs organizations an average of 9.2% of their annual revenue. At a $10 billion company, that represents $920 million in annual value leakage — not from bad strategy or poor products, but from an inability to read, track, and act on the terms that govern every commercial relationship the company has.

And yet contracts remain, in most enterprises, a dark data asset. Filed in document management systems, scattered across SharePoint instances, trapped in email archives, stored in the filing cabinets — literal and digital — of legal departments that treat them as compliance artifacts rather than operational intelligence.

The data infrastructure community has spent the better part of two decades building increasingly sophisticated systems to unify and activate customer data, and has largely ignored the data layer that governs how the enterprise actually transacts.

I think this is about to change — rapidly — and the implications for product marketers are significant.

The NLP Barrier and Its Collapse

The reason contracts remained dark for so long is linguistic, not strategic. Enterprise contracts are written in what may be the most adversarial prose form in business — language engineered by lawyers to be precise enough for enforcement and ambiguous enough for defensible interpretation at the same time.

Extracting structured, reliable data from a master services agreement with seventeen amendments negotiated over six years, across multiple jurisdictions, in three languages, was a problem that pre-LLM natural language processing could not solve at enterprise scale. Narrow extraction models existed — several CLM vendors built them for specific clause types — but coverage was too thin and error rates too high for general counsel or procurement leadership to trust the output for material decisions.

Large language models changed the economics of this problem in a way that is worth understanding precisely, because it has direct parallels to how earlier technology shifts unlocked adjacent enterprise data categories.

When foundation models demonstrated the ability to parse legal language with contextual understanding — not keyword matching, but genuine comprehension of how a proposed change to an indemnification cap in section 14.3 interacts with the limitation of liability in section 12.1 — contract data became extractable, normalizable, and queryable at scale for the first time.

🤖 The AI Agent Architecture for CLM

  • Extraction agents convert unstructured documents into structured metadata across hundreds of fields
  • Redline agents compare counterparty edits against negotiation playbooks and flag deviations at the clause level
  • Obligation agents track commitments and trigger compliance escalations
  • Search agents answer natural language queries across entire contract portfolios

The architectural pattern here should look familiar to anyone who lived through the CDP wave — but the more precise analogy may be what has happened since. CDPs solved a first-generation problem: unifying fragmented customer data into a single, addressable profile. That was necessary and valuable, but it was also contained — a customer data silo that replaced many smaller customer data silos.

The enterprise data architecture has since evolved past that model. Modern data fabric architectures — the kind being built by Snowflake, Databricks, SAP with its Business Data Cloud, and the broader lakehouse ecosystem — operate on a different premise: that enterprise data should not be copied and consolidated into a single platform but rather connected, governed, and made queryable across domains through a shared semantic layer.

The semantic layer is what allows a finance analyst and a supply chain planner and a marketing strategist to query the same underlying data using concepts that map to their respective domains — "customer lifetime value," "supplier lead time," "campaign attribution" — without each team maintaining its own extract. It is, in effect, a shared business vocabulary layered over a distributed data estate.

The Missing Domain

This is where contract data becomes not just useful but structurally essential.

A semantic layer is only as rich as the domains it can represent. Today, most enterprise semantic models cover financial data, customer data, product data, and operational data. Contractual data — the terms, obligations, pricing structures, and relationship logic encoded in millions of enterprise agreements — is almost entirely absent.

And without it, the semantic layer has a gap at its center: it can tell you what a customer bought, but not what you contractually committed to deliver. It can tell you what a supplier invoiced, but not whether that invoice aligns with the negotiated terms. It can surface a renewal date, but not the obligation schedule or penalty structure that gives that date its commercial significance.

The knowledge graph has the nodes. It's missing the edges.

The implication extends further when you consider knowledge graphs — the data structures that enterprises are increasingly using to represent complex, multi-hop relationships across their data estate. A knowledge graph can model how a customer relates to a contract, how that contract relates to a set of obligations, how those obligations relate to supplier performance metrics, and how those metrics relate to risk exposure. But only if contractual data is structured, extracted, and integrated into the graph.

Without it, the knowledge graph has edges missing between its most important nodes. The enterprise knows its customers and its suppliers and its products — but cannot computationally represent the agreements that bind them together.

This is what the leading CLM platforms are beginning to provide: not just a contract repository or a workflow tool, but a structured contract data layer that can feed the semantic models and knowledge graphs powering the next generation of enterprise analytics and AI.

The technology that unlocked CDPs was the convergence of cloud-scale data processing with identity resolution. The technology unlocking contract intelligence is the convergence of LLMs with domain-specific AI agents. But the destination is the same — making a previously dark data asset legible to the enterprise's broader data architecture.

The Market Structure

The CLM category is at an inflection point that maps closely to where CDPs were circa 2018-2019 — analyst recognition is strong, category-defining acquisitions are underway, and the buyer base is expanding beyond the initial departmental use case into cross-functional enterprise deployments.

Gartner has published a Magic Quadrant for CLM for four consecutive years. Forrester published its first CLM Wave in Q1 2025. The category leaders — Sirion, Icertis, Ironclad, Agiloft — have attracted significant institutional capital, with Sirion completing a majority investment from Haveli Investments (founded by Vista Equity Partners co-founder Brian Sheth) at an approximately $1 billion valuation in early 2026, and Icertis maintaining strategic relationships with SAP and Microsoft.

Market sizing estimates vary by scope definition, but the trajectory is consistent across analyst firms: the CLM market is projected to grow from approximately $1.8 billion in 2025 to $3.3-5.4 billion by 2030-2036, representing a 12-14% CAGR — with several analysts noting that AI-native platforms are growing considerably faster than the category average.

Perhaps more telling is the platform alliance structure that has emerged. SAP has aligned with Icertis. Salesforce has invested in Ironclad. Oracle has deepened its relationship with Sirion. This pattern — major enterprise platform vendors partnering with CLM specialists rather than building internally — mirrors the early CDP landscape, where Salesforce acquired Krux and later Datorama, Adobe acquired Marketo and later acquired TubeMogul, and Oracle acquired BlueKai and later Grapeshot.

The alliance-to-acquisition pipeline in enterprise software is well established, and CLM appears to be following the same trajectory.

The Product Marketing Challenge

For readers of The Future of PMM, the CLM category presents one of the more instructive go-to-market challenges in enterprise software right now — and it is instructive precisely because it recapitulates a challenge that many of us have faced before.

The core GTM problem is category perception. CLM is widely understood as legal technology — a workflow tool for lawyers. This perception is both partially accurate and strategically limiting. Contract creation, review, and negotiation are indeed core legal functions. But the data that contracts contain — pricing terms, SLA commitments, rebate thresholds, renewal conditions, obligation schedules, risk allocations — is operational data that procurement, finance, sales, and compliance teams need to act on continuously.

The CLM vendors who will win the category are the ones whose product marketing organizations successfully reframe the buyer's understanding of what contract data is and who it serves.

This is the same reframing that defined the CDP category — though the architectural context has since evolved considerably. In 2017, the dominant buyer perception was that CDPs were "another marketing database" — a tool for email segmentation and audience building. The product marketers who succeeded in that category — and I was involved in this work at Salesforce after the Krux acquisition — were the ones who repositioned CDPs as enterprise data infrastructure: a unification layer for customer data that served marketing, analytics, advertising, and customer service at once.

The reframe expanded the buyer from "VP of Email Marketing" to "Chief Data Officer," expanded the budget from marketing technology to enterprise data architecture, and expanded the competitive set from point solutions to platform plays.

CDPs have since been largely absorbed into broader data platform strategies — the lakehouse, the data cloud, the data fabric — but the PMM pattern that created the category remains one of the clearest examples of successful enterprise repositioning in the last decade.

CLM is at the beginning of an analogous reframe. The buyer needs to shift from "General Counsel looking for a document repository" to "Chief Procurement Officer, CFO, and CIO looking for a contract intelligence platform." The budget needs to shift from legal department discretionary spend to enterprise data infrastructure investment. And the competitive narrative needs to shift from "we manage contracts better than the other CLM vendors" to "we provide the missing semantic domain in your enterprise data architecture — contractual intelligence that your knowledge graph, your analytics platform, and your AI agents all need to operate on."

The agentic AI layer makes this reframe both more compelling and more difficult. More compelling because autonomous AI agents operating on contract data — drafting, redlining, extracting, monitoring — represent a tangible demonstration of how contract intelligence creates enterprise value beyond document management. More difficult because the buyer anxiety associated with AI autonomy over legal documents is considerable, and the positioning work required to build trust without overpromising is among the most nuanced in enterprise software.

Where This Sits in the Stack

I have spent the last three books mapping successive layers of enterprise data architecture — audience data with DMPs, customer data with CDPs, and now operational and agentic data with what I've been calling the Agentic Management Platform. The gap in my own framework, which I did not fully appreciate until I began studying the CLM category in depth, is that contractual data is not merely adjacent to these layers. It is foundational to them.

Consider what an enterprise data fabric is supposed to do: connect data across domains, apply governance and access controls, and expose a unified semantic model that business users and AI systems can query without needing to understand the underlying infrastructure.

Now consider what contracts represent within that fabric. A contract is not a single data point — it is a dense graph of relationships between entities (parties, subsidiaries, counterparties), obligations (SLAs, delivery schedules, compliance requirements), financial terms (pricing, rebates, penalties, escalation clauses), and temporal logic (effective dates, renewal windows, termination conditions). Each of those nodes connects to other enterprise data domains — the customer in the CRM, the supplier in the procurement system, the line item in the ERP, the performance metric in the analytics platform.

Without the contract layer, these connections exist in the minds of the lawyers and procurement managers who negotiated the deals, but not in the data architecture. The knowledge graph has the nodes. It's missing the edges.

This is why I believe contract intelligence platforms will eventually be understood not as a vertical application for legal departments but as a horizontal data layer — a first-class domain within the enterprise data fabric, with its own semantic model, its own governance requirements, and its own integration surfaces into the platforms that consume it.

The product marketers who position it that way — as the missing domain in the enterprise's semantic architecture — will define a category considerably larger than the one the analyst firms are currently sizing.

And the enterprises that integrate contractual data into their knowledge graphs and semantic models will have a structural advantage that their competitors, still treating contracts as documents rather than data, will find very difficult to replicate.

Chris O'Hara is the author of Data Driven (McGraw Hill, 2018), Customer Data Platforms (Wiley, 2020), and the forthcoming Agent Driven. He writes about the future of product marketing at futureofpmm.com and the future of enterprise software at thefullstack.chrisohara.com.
